title: Field Guide to Quiet Automation
subtitle: Small local systems that help without taking over
description: This sample book explores how local-first automation tools can stay understandable, reversible, and calm for the people using them.

cheatSheet.reminder: Instrument the work before you automate it.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this before adding a new helper or shortcut.

cheatSheet.reminder: Favor one helpful feedback cycle over a large hidden pipeline.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this when an automation starts to feel opaque.

cheatSheet.reminder: Show what the tool changed, what it skipped, and what still needs attention.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this when readers or operators must trust the outcome.

cheatSheet.reminder: Make saved data readable enough that a person can recover without the app.
cheatSheet.useWhen: Use this when local-first data may outlive the UI.

appendix.title: Automation heuristics
items: Prefer local persistence when the workflow is personal and offline-friendly.
Show status before optimization so trust arrives before speed.
Keep schemas stable so saved work survives interface changes.

appendix.title: Failure questions
items: What happens if the tool cannot finish?
Can a human inspect the saved state?
Is the next repair step obvious from the interface?

chapters.title: Listen before build
chapters.summary: Automation stays calm when it starts by observing the work it will later support.

sections.heading: Map the recurring requests
sections.lede: The best candidate for automation is not the loudest complaint but the repeatable request with a stable shape.
body: When you watch a workflow before automating it, you notice which steps are durable and which ones change depending on context. That keeps the first helper small and useful.
For local tools, this also helps define the data shape you need to store. Stable structure is what makes offline recovery possible.

callout.label: Pattern
callout.text: Map the recurring request first, then automate the handoff around it.

links.label: Design a tiny helper
links.reason: Observed patterns are easier to support with a focused local tool.

sections.heading: Collect the exceptions
sections.lede: Exceptions reveal the boundaries of a safe automation much faster than the happy path does.
body: A workflow may look simple until you catalogue the moments where a person pauses, checks context, or makes a judgment call. Those are often the moments that deserve visibility rather than full automation.
Capturing exceptions early prevents the schema from becoming too optimistic and brittle.

links.label: Make status visible
links.reason: Exceptions are easier to manage when the interface reports them clearly.

chapters.title: Tiny helpers
chapters.summary: A quiet system usually starts as a narrow helper with a clear boundary.

sections.heading: Start with local persistence
sections.lede: A personal workflow often benefits more from durability than from synchronization.
body: Local-first storage lets the tool keep context without asking the user to maintain accounts, permissions, or a remote service they do not really need.
This can be enough to support drafts, progress, bookmarks, and work queues while keeping the system easy to understand.

callout.label: Constraint
callout.text: Local-first does not mean throwaway. Exportable, stable data still matters.

links.label: Keep exports human-readable
links.reason: Local persistence is safer when the stored data can outlive the UI.

sections.heading: Name the thresholds
sections.lede: A helpful automation states what it will do automatically and what still needs review.
body: Thresholds reduce surprise. They tell the user when the helper can proceed independently and when it should pause for human judgment.
This creates a calmer relationship with automation because the boundary is visible in advance.

links.label: Show the current state
links.reason: Thresholds become trustworthy when the repair path is visible too.

chapters.title: Calm feedback
chapters.summary: Users trust an automation when it reports state, next steps, and failures without drama.

sections.heading: Show state, not mystery
sections.lede: Status should answer what changed, what is pending, and what the user can do next.
body: A calm interface does not bury the work inside a spinner or a vague success message. It explains enough that the person can decide whether to trust the result.
For local-first systems, this also makes refresh and recovery gentler because the important state is already visible on the surface.

callout.label: UX cue
callout.text: Visible state lowers anxiety more effectively than celebratory animation.

links.label: Review the observed exceptions
links.reason: Status is most helpful when it mirrors the real exceptions in the workflow.

sections.heading: Make repair paths obvious
sections.lede: Every failure message should imply a next step instead of ending the workflow.
body: The user needs to know whether to retry, inspect data, adjust an input, or accept partial completion. Repair options are part of the interface, not an afterthought.
This is especially important when no backend exists, because the user and the app are sharing the same device-level context.

links.label: Steward the saved data
links.reason: Repair gets easier when the underlying state is stable and inspectable.

chapters.title: Stewarding local systems
chapters.summary: Local-first tools stay healthy when their saved data remains legible and stable over time.

sections.heading: Keep exports human-readable
sections.lede: A local system becomes safer when a person can inspect the saved state without the full interface.
body: Readable JSON makes debugging, migration, and recovery less fragile. It also makes AI-assisted generation easier because the structure stays explicit.
This is one reason schemas matter even for small personal PWAs: they make change safer.

callout.label: Rule
callout.text: If a saved file cannot be understood by a person, future maintenance becomes much harder.

links.label: Revisit local persistence
links.reason: Readable exports make local-first storage more durable.

sections.heading: Evolve the schema carefully
sections.lede: Stable IDs and predictable shapes are the quiet infrastructure behind trustworthy local tools.
body: When a schema changes abruptly, saved progress and bookmarks often break first. Small, additive changes preserve continuity for the user.
Treat the schema as part of the product experience: it shapes whether the tool feels dependable across versions.

links.label: Return to feedback design
links.reason: Schema changes should remain visible and understandable from the interface.
